title: NordVPN Review 2026: Is It Still the Best VPN?
excerpt: NordVPN has dominated VPN recommendations for years — but is it still the right choice in 2026? This in-depth review covers performance, privacy policy, security features, pricing, and how it compares to alternatives. We also address the 2018 breach and what Nord has done since.
content:
NordVPN in 2026: A Quick Verdict

After testing NordVPN across Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android, the short answer is: yes, it remains one of the top picks for most users. It's fast, its privacy infrastructure has been significantly hardened since the 2018 incident, and it bundles useful extras — including dark web monitoring and a password manager — at a competitive price.

That said, it's not perfect, and it's not the right choice for every situation. This review covers what NordVPN does well, where it falls short, and who should consider an alternative.

Try NordVPN →

Performance: Speed and Reliability

NordVPN's NordLynx protocol (built on WireGuard) is consistently among the fastest in independent speed tests. In 2025 and early 2026 tests by AV-TEST and PCMag, NordVPN reduced download speeds by an average of 20–30% on nearby servers — acceptable for streaming and video calls — and showed strong performance on long-distance connections compared to OpenVPN-based competitors.

Server count: NordVPN operates 6,400+ servers across 111 countries as of early 2026. That breadth means you can usually find a fast, nearby server, and it helps with unblocking geo-restricted content (Netflix, BBC iPlayer, Disney+, and most major streaming platforms work reliably).

Specialty servers: Double VPN (traffic routed through two servers), Onion over VPN (for Tor users), and Obfuscated servers (for restrictive network environments like corporate firewalls or certain countries) are available at no extra cost.

Privacy and Security Architecture

Privacy is where VPN reputation really matters, and NordVPN has made verifiable structural improvements since 2018.

No-logs policy: NordVPN has a strict no-logs policy — independently audited by PricewaterhouseCoopers (twice) and Deloitte. These audits verify that Nord's servers don't store connection logs, IP addresses, traffic data, or DNS queries. The audits are meaningful precisely because they're done by major accounting/auditing firms, not by Nord itself.

RAM-only servers: As of 2020, NordVPN migrated to diskless RAM-only servers. This means that even if law enforcement seizes physical hardware, there is no persistent data to recover. Every reboot wipes all data.

Jurisdiction: NordVPN is headquartered in Panama, which has no mandatory data-retention laws and is outside the 5/9/14 Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances. This matters for users concerned about government surveillance.

Kill switch: Available on all platforms. If the VPN connection drops, the kill switch cuts your internet rather than letting traffic leak over your regular connection.

DNS leak protection: NordVPN routes all DNS requests through its own servers, preventing DNS leaks that could reveal your browsing activity to your ISP.

The 2018 Breach: What Happened and What Changed

In 2019, NordVPN disclosed that a third-party data center in Finland had been compromised in 2018. An attacker exploited an insecure remote management system left by the data center provider and gained brief access to one server.

No user credentials or activity logs were exposed (because Nord doesn't store them), but the breach raised legitimate questions about Nord's vendor oversight. Nord's response was to: terminate contracts with non-compliant data centers, deploy full-disk encryption across its server network, migrate to RAM-only servers, commission multiple independent security audits, and launch a bug bounty program.

Whether you consider that response adequate is a judgment call. The structural changes — RAM-only servers, independent audits, tighter vendor standards — are meaningful. The 2022 and 2024 Deloitte audits found no evidence of logging.

Extra Features Worth Knowing About

Threat Protection Pro: A built-in ad blocker, malware scanner, and tracker blocker. It works independently of the VPN tunnel, so you get protection even when you're not connected to a VPN server. In testing, it blocked most known malvertising and tracking scripts.

NordPass bundling: NordVPN's Complete plan includes NordPass (their password manager) and NordLocker (encrypted cloud storage). If you're looking for a password manager anyway, the bundle can offer good value. For a standalone password manager comparison, see our best LastPass alternatives.

NordProtect: Nord's identity protection service (available as an add-on or in the Complete plan) provides dark web monitoring, credit monitoring, and identity theft insurance in markets where available. See our dark web monitoring guide for context on how these services work.

Meshnet: A free feature that lets you create a private encrypted network between your own devices — useful for accessing home systems remotely or creating a secure network with trusted people without a dedicated server.

Pricing in 2026

NordVPN's pricing structure (as of 2026):

  • Basic (1 month): ~$12.99/month
  • Basic (1 year): ~$4.59/month — most popular for individual users
  • Basic (2 years): ~$3.09/month
  • Plus: Adds NordPass password manager
  • Complete: Adds NordPass + NordLocker (encrypted storage)

The 2-year plan offers the best per-month value but requires a multi-year commitment. Nord frequently runs promotional pricing, so the actual price at purchase may differ. All plans cover 10 simultaneous devices.

NordVPN offers a 30-day money-back guarantee with no questions asked, which makes it low-risk to test.

Who Should Use NordVPN (and Who Shouldn't)

Good fit for: Users who want a reliable all-rounder for streaming, torrenting, and general privacy. People who travel and use public Wi-Fi regularly. Users who want a single subscription that bundles a VPN, password manager, and identity protection. Beginners who want a polished app with minimal configuration.

Consider alternatives if: You need maximum anonymity for high-risk activities (consider Mullvad, which accepts cash and requires no email). You're in a country where VPN use is heavily restricted and need maximum obfuscation. You prioritize open-source transparency above all else (ProtonVPN publishes full client source code). You only need a VPN occasionally and don't want a subscription.

For VPN use specifically to protect your work connection, see our guide on VPN for remote work.

The Bottom Line

NordVPN in 2026 is a polished, fast, audited VPN from a company that has demonstrably improved its security practices after a significant incident. The no-logs policy is independently verified, the infrastructure is hardened, and the feature set is competitive.

It's not the cheapest, and it's not the most privacy-focused option for adversarial threat models. But for the majority of users — people who want reliable privacy on public Wi-Fi, streaming access, and basic identity protection — it's a strong choice.
